text: Does direct (N, phi) evolution succeed? At what point does the quantum pressure (sqrt(N))_xx / (2 sqrt(N)) singularity affect accuracy or stability? What is the failure mode if you do NOT use Madelung-Psi transform?

method: Madelung-Psi split-step Fourier (baseline reference): Psi = sqrt(N)*exp(i*phi); split Psi_t = i*Psi_xx/2 + i*kappa*|Psi|^2 Psi via Strang splitting

rationale: Strong contrast: Madelung-Psi is machine-precision exact while direct (N, phi) with eps=0 blows up in 1 RK4 step. The mechanism is exactly the quantum pressure (sqrt(N))_xx / (2*sqrt(N)) singularity in the exponential soliton tails (min(N0) ~ 3e-24, leading to |Q| ~ 1/sqrt(N) ~ 1e12 amplification through k^2). Confirms hypothesis that direct (N, phi) is unusable without regularization.

text: Try direct (N, phi) with regularization eps=1e-6 and eps=1e-3 inside sqrt(N) (i.e., Q computed with sqrt(max(N, eps))). Does it stabilize? Does it preserve mass / soliton shape? This characterizes the regularization-vs-physics tradeoff.

method: Direct (N, phi) spectral FFT + RK4 with regularization sqrt(max(N, eps)) in quantum pressure; tested eps in {1e-12, 1e-6, 1e-3}.

rationale: Hard-floor regularization sqrt(max(N, eps)) does NOT save the direct method, even at eps=1e-3 (which is 0.05% of peak). The hard floor introduces a non-smooth step in sqrt(N), whose second spectral derivative is Gibbs-oscillatory and grows without bound. Counter-intuitively, eps=1e-3 has the LARGEST qmax (5.2e12), worse than eps=0 (6.8e7), because the larger flat-region size amplifies the spectral artifact. Survival improves from blowup-at-step-1 to blowup-at-step-6 — still 4 orders of magnitude short of T=4.0 (4000 steps).

text: Test smooth regularization Nsafe = N + eps (no step) at the same eps levels and contrast against hard-floor. Hypothesis: smoothness, not magnitude, is what matters for direct (N, phi). This is the third experiment.

method: Direct (N, phi) RK4 spectral with SMOOTH regularization Nsafe = N + eps (no max() step), eps in {1e-6, 1e-3, 1e-1}; plus a stronger filter (2/3-rule dealiasing) variant.

rationale: Smooth regularization Nsafe = N + eps DOES extend survival monotonically (eps=1e-6: 2 steps; 1e-3: 5 steps; 1e-1: 13 steps), and 2/3-rule dealiasing adds another factor (21 steps). BUT all variants still blow up well before T=4.0 (need 4000 steps). The deepest cause is structural: the (N, phi) PDE has a (sqrt(N))_xx coupling that creates an unstable mode whenever sqrt(N) is not analytic on the grid; spectral derivatives amplify any non-smooth feature, and explicit RK4 has no damping. The dispersive (Schrodinger) structure is fundamentally linear in Psi but non-Lipschitz in (N, phi) at N=0. Conclusion: Madelung-Psi is not just convenient — it is mathematically necessary on a periodic spectral discretization of a focusing bright soliton whose tails decay below machine epsilon.

text: Stop. The (method x regularization) phase diagram has been mapped within the 3-experiment budget. Madelung-Psi: machine precision. Direct (N, phi): blowup in 1-21 steps regardless of eps in {0, 1e-12, 1e-6, 1e-3, 1e-1} or hard/soft regularization or 2/3-rule dealiasing. Final candidate.py reflects the Madelung-Psi method (best-working).
